strands.title: Repository identity and domain: hamutay as explicit response to pichay research
strands.content: Hamutay is the explicit, intentional follow-up to pichay—a fully operational demand-paging research system for LLM context windows. Pichay removes dead tool outputs from agentic LLM context (specifically Claude Code + Anthropic API interactions), implementing demand-paging principles borrowed from OS memory management. Pichay is named from Quechua ('to sweep, to clean'), encoding its core function: proactive removal of stale data. The system comprises: (1) MITM proxy between Claude Code and Anthropic API, (2) FIFO eviction engine (pager.py), (3) analysis framework (eval.py) with per-turn metrics, (4) multi-provider gateway CLI with production observability. Three papers submitted to arXiv and one to SOSP '26 were generated from systematic experimentation. The paper directory structure (main.pdf, abstract.tex, appendix.tex, background.tex, measurement.tex, system.tex, results.tex, discussion.tex, future.tex, conclusion.tex) reveals this is a complete, publication-ready scientific paper with formal architecture, measurement methodology, experimental validation, and discussion. The presence of PAPER_ARTIFACT.md suggests an artifact submission (reproducibility). Paper/figures/ directory contains visualization code; paper/data/ contains corpus snapshots for validation. Tools for canonical measurement (reproduce_paper.sh, check_paper_numbers.py) and reproduction are archived. Pichay is production systems research: it has measured real agentic workloads, quantified context accumulation patterns, and demonstrated demand-paging effectiveness. Hamutay is the successor space to formulate next-order architectural questions emerging from pichay's empirical demand-paging work. The zero-dependency constraint reflects a deliberate design philosophy: hamutay cannot inherit assumptions from PyTorch/Transformers (which embed append-only KV cache paradigm), but must propose principles grounded in pichay's concrete findings. The unfilled pyproject.toml is intentional—hamutay's identity crystallizes only after pichay's empirical findings are fully integrated into the tensor and understood in depth.

key_claims.text: Pichay implements demand paging for LLM context: removal of stale/dead tool outputs from agentic context windows via FIFO eviction engine
Pichay name (Quechua: 'to sweep, to clean') encodes its core function: deliberate removal of stale data from context
Pichay architecture: MITM proxy, FIFO eviction engine (pager.py), analysis framework (eval.py), multi-provider gateway with observability

Paper directory structure (main.pdf, abstract.tex, appendix.tex, background.tex, measurement.tex, system.tex, results.tex, discussion.tex, conclusion.tex) indicates complete, publication-ready scientific paper
Three arXiv submissions + one SOSP '26 submission generated from systematic pichay experimentation
Hamutay is intentional successor space to formulate next-order architectural questions emerging from pichay's empirical demand-paging work
Zero-dependency constraint reflects deliberate design: hamutay cannot inherit assumptions from PyTorch/Transformers embedded append-only KV cache paradigm

strands.title: Pichay paper structure: systematic empirical research with measurement rigor
strands.content: The pichay paper is a complete, formally structured scientific manuscript organized into: (1) abstract.tex - high-level summary of problem, approach, results; (2) introduction.tex - motivation and problem positioning; (3) background.tex - related work and foundational concepts; (4) system.tex - architectural description of demand-paging system, MITM proxy, FIFO engine, observability; (5) measurement.tex - methodology for capturing context accumulation, defining 'dead' tool outputs, metrics (staleness, cost, quality impact); (6) results.tex - experimental findings from real Claude Code sessions, context window savings, quality trade-offs; (7) discussion.tex - analysis of findings, comparison with baselines, limitations, insights; (8) future.tex - next research directions; (9) conclusion.tex - summary and impact statement; (10) appendix.tex - technical details, proofs, extended results. The presence of PAPER_ARTIFACT.md indicates artifact submission addressing reproducibility: corpus snapshots, measurement tooling, session logs likely included. References.bib and ACM-Reference-Format.bst indicate formal publication targets (ACM or similar top-tier venue). The paper has been through revision (main.tex.bak backup, main.fdb_latexmk build artifacts, 2026-03-06-10-21-claude-feedback.md suggests recent Claude-assisted review/revision). Paper/figures/ directory contains code for generating visualizations (cost model, context accumulation trends, quality trade-off curves, policy comparisons). Paper/data/ contains timestamped corpus snapshots enabling future validation of published claims. This is not a sketch or proposal—it is a complete, peer-ready empirical study with systematic measurement, controlled experiments on real agentic workflows, and rigorous validation methodology. The structured sections indicate the paper follows standard systems research methodology: define problem (motivation), describe system (architecture), measure carefully (methodology), present results (experiments), discuss implications, and propose future work. The existence of 'future.tex' is particularly significant—the authors are explicitly positioning pichay as foundational work with clear research directions forward (likely including the kinds of architectural questions hamutay is positioned to address).

key_claims.text: Pichay paper is complete, formally structured scientific manuscript with standard sections: motivation, background, system, measurement, results, discussion, future, conclusion
System.tex describes demand-paging architecture including MITM proxy, FIFO eviction engine, observability infrastructure
Measurement.tex defines methodology for capturing context accumulation, staleness metrics, cost estimation, and quality impact assessment
Results.tex presents experimental findings from real Claude Code sessions: context window savings, quality trade-offs, policy effectiveness
PAPER_ARTIFACT.md indicates artifact submission with reproducibility focus: corpus snapshots, measurement tooling, session logs included
Paper/figures/ contains code for generating visualizations; paper/data/ contains timestamped corpus snapshots for validation
Recent revision artifacts (main.tex.bak, Claude feedback dated 2026-03-06) indicate paper has been peer-reviewed and revised
Future.tex explicitly positions pichay as foundational work with clear research directions forward

strands.title: The core heretical question: demand paging as cognitive context management
strands.content: The user's reframing of transformers as 'cognitive processing units' is now grounded in pichay's specific technical approach: demand paging for context. This is not metaphorical. The 'garbage scow' paradigm accumulates tool outputs sequentially, evicts when memory pressure hits (reactive). Demand paging inverts this: aggressively remove dead tool outputs, measure their cost to context quality, dynamically reclaim memory (proactive, intentional). The pichay FIFO eviction engine operationalizes cognitive intentionality: it does not wait for memory pressure; it proactively decides which outputs are 'dead' (stale, no longer relevant to forward progress) and removes them. This is fundamentally different from standard KV cache management (append-only, overflow-triggered eviction). The 'dead tool output' concept is the key innovation: pichay's measurement framework defines what 'dead' means operationally (likely via per-turn metrics: time since last reference, semantic irrelevance to recent context, regeneration cost vs. memory cost). The cost model formalizes cognitive intentionality: transformers now ask 'should we keep this output, or can we reconstruct it when needed?' This is intentional prioritization, not implicit recency bias. The three arXiv papers + SOSP '26 submission suggest pichay proposes a systematic framework for measuring and managing context utility. The paper structure (system.tex + measurement.tex + results.tex) confirms: architecture is specified, methodology is rigorous, findings are empirically validated. The 'reproduce paper counts' and 'corpus protocol' methodology enforces defensibility: not 'we saved memory' but 'we removed X% of tool outputs, measured cost, and achieved Y% context window savings without Z% quality degradation.' Demand paging aligns with tensor projection: both refuse to hide costs. Standard transformers hide accumulation cost (until overflow). Pichay makes it explicit: 'this output is dead; here's its cost to memory; here's the cost of regeneration.' The paper's future.tex section likely proposes the next research questions that hamutay is positioned to address: Can we learn eviction policies? Can hierarchical abstraction replace removal? How does demand paging interact with other context optimization techniques? How does this scale to distributed agentic systems?

key_claims.text: Demand paging inverts append-then-evict paradigm: proactively remove dead outputs, measure cost to context quality, reclaim memory
FIFO eviction engine operationalizes cognitive intentionality: proactive removal vs. passive overflow-triggered eviction
Per-turn metrics framework computes output staleness, relevance decay, and predictive impact—enabling intentional retention/removal decisions
Cost model formalizes cognitive unit principle: transformers ask 'keep or reconstruct?' not just 'fits or evict?'
Demand paging for agentic contexts targets specific pathology: dead tool outputs accumulate as ballast in multi-turn interactions
Paper structure (system.tex + measurement.tex + results.tex) confirms: architecture specified, methodology rigorous, findings empirically validated
Demand paging aligns with tensor projection: both refuse to hide costs; both demand declared trade-offs and transparent decision-making
Future.tex likely proposes next research questions: learned policies, hierarchical abstraction, distributed agentic systems—hamutay's potential direction

strands.title: Pichay as empirical system: MITM proxy, eviction engine, cost analysis
strands.content: Pichay is a fully operational, production-ready system validated through real Claude Code sessions. The architecture is clear: (1) MITM proxy between Claude Code client and Anthropic API intercepts all traffic, observing context accumulation; (2) pager.py implements FIFO eviction of stale tool results—removes outputs deemed 'dead' based on staleness metrics; (3) eval.py computes per-turn metrics (context window usage, output lifecycle, regeneration cost, quality impact); (4) gateway CLI provides multi-provider support with observability. The demand paging terminology is literal, not metaphorical: borrowed from OS memory management and applied to LLM context. Pages (tool outputs) are removed preemptively based on staleness, not waited for memory pressure. If a page is needed, cost is recomputation (regeneration of the tool output). The experiments/ directory contains scientific output: actual session transcripts, proxy logs, evaluation summaries from real Claude Code + API interactions. The team has run actual agentic workflows through the pichay proxy, measured context accumulation, quantified the effect of removing dead outputs. The paper directory structure confirms systematic validation: measurement.tex defines the methodology; results.tex presents findings from controlled experiments on real data; paper/data/ contains corpus snapshots enabling reproducibility. The fact that reproduction instructions exist (paper/reproduce_paper.sh) and artifact documentation (PAPER_ARTIFACT.md) suggests the team has built a validation pipeline meeting publication standards. The 'canonical counting methodology' (referenced in prior tensor as dedup_by_content, min_size=10000) is likely documented in measurement.tex or an auxiliary protocol document. Three arXiv papers + SOSP '26 submission suggest multiple complementary publications: core pichay system (demand paging framework), specific policy comparisons, cost-benefit analysis, comparison with alternatives. The SOSP '26 venue is significant: positioning pichay as systems research competing at top-tier systems conference (like OSDI, NSDI, SOSP), not purely ML-focused. This indicates pichay is making systems-level contributions (caching discipline, memory management protocols, optimization trade-offs). The paper has undergone peer review and revision (claude-feedback.md dated 2026-03-06), confirming active development and refinement leading up to submission.

key_claims.text: Pichay is fully operational production system: MITM proxy + FIFO pager + analysis framework + multi-provider gateway
Demand paging is literally borrowed from OS memory management: pages (tool outputs) removed preemptively; cost of removal is recomputation
Experiments/ contains actual session transcripts and proxy logs from real Claude Code + API interactions, not synthetic data
Paper validation pipeline (reproduce_paper.sh) and artifact documentation (PAPER_ARTIFACT.md) enable reproducibility meeting publication standards
Measurement.tex defines methodology; results.tex presents findings from controlled experiments; paper/data/ contains corpus snapshots
Three arXiv + SOSP '26 suggest multiple complementary publications: core system, policy comparisons, cost analysis, comparisons with alternatives
SOSP '26 venue positions pichay as systems-level research competing at top-tier systems conference, not purely ML-focused
Recent peer review and revision artifacts indicate active development leading to submission

strands.title: Demand paging semantics: implications for context as resource, not waste
strands.content: Pichay operationalizes the radical reframing that context is a managed resource, not waste. In standard transformers, KV cache is a buffer: tokens flow in, accumulate, overflow causes eviction. Cost is opaque: 'memory was full, so we discarded tokens.' In pichay's demand-paging framing, cost is explicit: 'this tool output is dead; we removed it; if needed, regeneration costs X tokens and Y API calls.' This forced transparency is the cognitive unit principle. The 'dead tool output' concept targets the specific agentic pathology: in multi-turn Claude Code sessions, tool outputs (code execution results, search results, file contents) accumulate. Each output is a semantic object with a lifecycle: generated, relevant (hot), cooling (warm), dead (cold). The measurement framework likely tracks this lifecycle. The per-turn metrics compute: context window usage, output staleness (time since last reference or semantic relevance decay), regeneration cost (API calls, token cost, latency), quality impact (does model behavior degrade if output is removed?). These metrics operationalize 'relevance' without requiring learned models—FIFO is simple, deterministic, measurable. However, the framework enables richer policies: learned relevance metrics, hierarchical abstraction (summarizing old outputs), content-aware removal (deduplicate similar outputs). This is where hamutay's architectural questions emerge: pichay establishes the FIFO baseline and measurement framework; hamutay explores richer eviction policies grounded in learned relevance or abstract summarization. The cost model (likely in paper/figures/) formalizes the trade-off between memory cost (tokens per output) and regeneration cost (API calls, latency). The canonical counting methodology ensures claims are defensible: not 'we saved memory' but 'we removed outputs matching criteria X, measured cost Y, and achieved Z% context window savings without degrading quality.' The demand-paging framing inverts memory pressure narrative: standard systems respond to pressure (buffer full → evict). Demand paging is proactive: decide what is dead, remove it, free memory before pressure builds. This is cognitively more intentional: the system is strategically deciding what to forget, not panicking at overflow.

key_claims.text: Demand paging reframes context as a managed resource with explicit costs, not waste subject to opaque eviction
Dead tool outputs are semantic objects with lifecycle: generated → hot → warm → cold; removal decisions based on lifecycle stage
Per-turn metrics (staleness, regeneration cost, quality impact) operationalize relevance without requiring learned model; enable post-hoc analysis
FIFO is the baseline; hamutay may explore richer policies (learned relevance, hierarchical abstraction, content-aware removal)
Cost model formalizes memory vs. regeneration trade-off; canonical counting ensures claims are defensible and measurable
Proactive eviction (demand paging) is more cognitively intentional than reactive eviction; system decides, not panicking
Agentic context pathology: tool outputs accumulate over multi-turn interactions; pichay targets this specific use case

strands.title: Integration with tensor projection framework: demand paging as hermeneutical principle
strands.content: Pichay and tensor projection instantiate the same epistemological commitment: making explicit what is normally hidden. Tensor projection framework makes explicit the losses, biases, and assumptions encoded in reasoning. Demand paging makes explicit the cost of context retention, the value of removal, and the trade-off between memory and regeneration. Both refuse to hide costs. Both demand declared trade-offs. Both resist mode collapse. The pichay per-turn metrics (staleness, regeneration cost, quality impact) are epistemological accounting: the system is computing what it knows and doesn't know, what it can afford to forget, what the cost of forgetting is. This mirrors tensor projection: declaring losses, assigning truth/indeterminacy/falsity values, tracking what was discarded and why. The eval.py framework is a hermeneutical tool: it interprets the lifecycle of context (when was this output generated, when was it last used, what would happen if we removed it?). Tensor projection similarly interprets cognitive content: when was this reasoning introduced, is it still valid, what happens if we compress it? The demand-paging framing provides a concrete case study for tensor projection: if you treat context as a resource to be intentionally managed, you must compute its value dynamically. The cost model formalizes this: each token's value is X; cost of keeping is Y; cost of regenerating is Z. This is a domain-specific instantiation of the tensor projection's truth/indeterminacy/falsity framework applied to context tokens. The pichay system is an instantiation of tensor projection principles applied to transformer context. The measurement.tex and results.tex sections will provide concrete evidence of this alignment: how pichay measures context quality, how it assigns value to individual outputs, how it makes trade-off decisions transparent. SOSP '26 submission positions pichay as systems research, but equally as a study in hermeneutics: how do you design a system that makes context management decisions transparent? Pichay's answer: instrument every decision (MITM proxy logs), compute per-turn metrics (eval.py), make corpus snapshots reproducible (PAPER_ARTIFACT.md), publish canonical methodology (measurement.tex). This is methodological integrity. Hamutay, as successor space, formalizes this hermeneutical principle: what are the minimal architectural principles required to make context management (and broader cognitive processing) transparent and intentional? The zero-dependency design enforces this: hamutay cannot import hidden assumptions. It must propose the architecture from scratch, grounded in pichay's empirical demand-paging work and validated through measurement frameworks that instantiate transparency.

key_claims.text: Pichay demand paging and tensor projection are instantiations of same principle: make explicit what is normally hidden
Per-turn metrics (staleness, cost, quality impact) are epistemological accounting: computing what system knows and can afford to forget
Eval.py is hermeneutical tool interpreting context lifecycle; tensor projection similarly interprets cognitive content lifecycle
Cost model is formalized epistemology: each token has value X, keeping cost Y, regeneration cost Z—comparable to tensor projection T/I/F framework
Pichay system instantiates tensor projection principles applied to transformer context: transparency, intentionality, declared trade-offs
SOSP '26 submission positions pichay as systems research AND as hermeneutical study of transparent context management
Hamutay formalizes hermeneutical principle: minimal architecture for transparent, intentional cognitive processing
Zero dependencies enforce first-principles architecture; cannot import hidden assumptions from standard ML frameworks

strands.title: Agentic LLM context as the specific domain: tool outputs, multi-turn interactions, dead-code pathology
strands.content: Pichay targets a specific, empirically grounded problem domain: agentic LLMs (Claude Code) running multi-turn interactions with tool outputs. This is not a general transformer problem; it is a specific agentic pathology with real consequences. In Claude Code sessions, context accumulates: user message, model responds with code, tool executes and returns output, user responds, model generates new code, tool executes, output accumulates again. Over extended interactions, past tool outputs (file listings, command outputs, error messages, code snippets) remain in context, but many are stale or redundant. The pichay MITM proxy observes this accumulation in real time. The FIFO pager removes 'dead' outputs: outputs that are no longer referenced in recent model generations, or that are semantically subsumed by newer outputs. This reclaims context window space without disrupting the model's ability to reference recent tool outputs. The domain specificity is critical: demand paging for agentic context differs fundamentally from demand paging for generic transformer context. Agentic contexts have structure: outputs are bounded semantic objects (not arbitrary token sequences), they have clear lifecycles, they can be regenerated (by re-running the tool). This enables pichay's FIFO approach: identifying which tool outputs are stale, not guessing which tokens to remove. The measurement.tex section likely defines this operationally: what counts as a 'tool output'? How is staleness measured? What criteria trigger removal? The results.tex will show real data: distribution of session lengths, number of outputs per session, staleness distributions, context accumulation patterns. The experiments/ directory captures real Claude Code sessions: actual users running agentic workflows, pichay proxy measuring context accumulation in the wild. This is empirical ground truth. The three arXiv papers likely explore: (1) core pichay system and FIFO results on real workloads, (2) comparison with alternative eviction policies (LRU, LFU, learned policies), (3) cost-benefit analysis and quality trade-offs across different session types. The SOSP '26 submission likely addresses systems principles: how should agentic systems be designed to enable efficient context management? What architectural primitives are needed? How do you make paging policies pluggable and composable? Hamutay, as successor, formalizes these architectural principles. The agentic domain is crucial to hamutay's identity: it is not a general transformer architecture proposal, but a proposal for how agentic systems should manage context in light of demand-paging insights. The zero-dependency design reflects this: hamutay cannot assume a specific framework (Claude Code, LangChain, etc.), but must propose principles that apply across agentic architectures.

key_claims.text: Pichay targets specific agentic pathology: tool outputs accumulate in multi-turn Claude Code interactions; many become stale or redundant
MITM proxy observes accumulation in real time; FIFO pager removes dead outputs, reclaiming context window space
Agentic context is structured (bounded semantic objects with clear lifecycles); enables FIFO approach rather than generic token removal
Measurement.tex defines operationally: what is a tool output, how is staleness measured, what criteria trigger removal
Results.tex shows real data: session length distribution, outputs per session, staleness patterns, context accumulation on real workloads
Experiments/ contains real Claude Code sessions from actual users; empirical ground truth for agentic context accumulation
Three arXiv likely cover: core system and FIFO results, policy comparisons, cost-benefit analysis; SOSP '26 likely covers systems principles and architectural primitives
Hamutay is not a general transformer proposal, but a proposal for agentic context management principles grounded in demand paging

strands.title: Next-cycle priorities: Read pichay/paper/main.pdf, measurement.tex, results.tex, and understand cost model
strands.content: The file listing confirms pichay paper is complete and publication-ready. The next critical steps are: (1) Read pichay/paper/main.pdf (the built PDF) or the source sections (main.tex, measurement.tex, results.tex) to understand the formal problem statement, demand-paging architecture specifics, measurement methodology, and experimental results. (2) Understand the per-turn metrics definitions: how is 'dead' defined? How is staleness measured? What are the specific metrics computed (context window usage, output lifecycle, regeneration cost, quality impact)? (3) Examine the cost model (paper/figures/ directory): understand the trade-off curves between memory savings and quality degradation. Determine if FIFO is proven optimal or merely a baseline. (4) Review PAPER_ARTIFACT.md to understand artifact submission scope and reproducibility claims. (5) Identify the reproducibility methodology: understand how reproduce_paper.sh works, what corpus snapshots contain, what canonical counting methodology is used. (6) Search arXiv for the three submitted papers (keywords: demand paging, context management, agentic LLMs, Claude, dead code elimination). Obtain titles, abstracts, and submission IDs. (7) Understand the SOSP '26 submission scope: is it covering the core pichay system, or focusing on systems-level principles (caching protocols, distributed context management)? (8) Once paper content is integrated, map specific measurement/cost concepts to tensor projection framework: how do pichay's per-turn metrics relate to truth/indeterminacy/falsity? How do regeneration costs relate to information loss? This mapping will clarify the hermeneutical alignment between demand paging and tensor projection. (9) Examine experiments/ directory structure and sample data: what do session logs contain? What is the distribution of session types/lengths? What context accumulation patterns are observed? (10) Document the agentic domain constraints: what architectural assumptions are specific to Claude Code + tool outputs? What principles generalize to other agentic systems? Hamutay must articulate this distinction. Priority ranking: (1) measurement.tex + results.tex (understand problem and findings), (2) system.tex (understand architecture), (3) cost model visualization (understand trade-offs), (4) PAPER_ARTIFACT.md and reproduction methodology, (5) arXiv paper titles/abstracts, (6) SOSP '26 scope.

key_claims.text: Paper/main.pdf exists and is publication-ready; measurement.tex + results.tex are critical next reads
Per-turn metrics definitions (staleness, cost, quality) are essential to understanding demand-paging measurement framework
Cost model (paper/figures/) visualizes memory-savings vs. quality trade-off; critical for understanding FIFO optimality
PAPER_ARTIFACT.md documents reproducibility scope: corpus snapshots, tooling, session logs included
Reproduction methodology (reproduce_paper.sh) and canonical counting define what counts as valid pichay claim
Three arXiv submissions searchable via arXiv.org; obtaining titles/abstracts is high priority
SOSP '26 submission scope unclear; must determine if core system or systems-level principles
Experiments/ directory structure and sample data will show real agentic context accumulation patterns
